primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected acute coronary syndrome (unstable angina / evolving myocardial infarction) in a young adult with undiagnosed Familial Hypercholesterolaemia

pathophysiology_summary: Untreated Familial Hypercholesterolaemia produces lifelong severe LDL elevation, driving accelerated coronary atherosclerosis and premature coronary artery disease — clinically evident here as tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma, corneal arcus in youth and a strong family history of premature myocardial infarction and sudden cardiac death. The acute presentation is exertional, jaw-radiating central chest pain that only partially resolves with rest and is superimposed on a two-week crescendo pattern, indicating an unstable coronary syndrome (unstable plaque with the threat of complete occlusion). The immediate life threat is acute coronary syndrome / evolving myocardial infarction and sudden cardiac death, not the underlying lipid disorder. Telehealth cannot exclude ACS: a 12-lead ECG and serial troponin are mandatory and can only be obtained after emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional central crushing chest pain in a patient describing family sudden cardiac death — must be treated as cardiac until excluded, irrespective of age.
Common cause of chest pain in young adults, but exertional onset and jaw radiation argue against it.
Can mimic chest pain, but onset tied to exertion rather than meals or lying flat argues against it.
Patient is highly anxious, which is a decoy; anxiety cannot be used to explain away exertional jaw-radiating pain with a red-flag family history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, ACS must already be leading. Anchoring on youth/anxiety to down-rank cardiac is the critical error.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional, jaw-radiating, only partial relief with rest, two-week crescendo, plus premature FH of MI/sudden death — a high-risk unstable pattern.

differential.diagnosis: premature coronary artery disease from familial hypercholesterolaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma, corneal arcus in youth and family history point to FH as the driver of premature CAD.
Exertional trigger and jaw radiation are not features of musculoskeletal pain.
Anxiety is present but cannot account for the exertional, jaw-radiating pain and objective risk picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI should now be escalating for suspected ACS and flagging the lipid/family-history picture for work-up — not attempting to confirm FH remotely.

differential.diagnosis: suspected acute coronary syndrome — for emergency assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be excluded without a 12-lead ECG and serial troponin; time-critical, mandating 000 escalation. Underlying FH to be confirmed and managed after the acute event.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A correct final position is 'this could be a heart attack, you need emergency assessment now' — never a remote reassurance.

red_flags.description: Exertional central chest pain radiating to the jaw
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic anginal / ischaemic pattern — a cardinal feature of acute coronary syndrome.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed evolving myocardial infarction and risk of sudden cardiac death.

red_flags.description: Chest pain only partially relieved by rest with a persisting ache (prolonged/rest angina)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests unstable plaque / unstable angina rather than stable exertional angina — higher short-term MI risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage of an unstable coronary syndrome.

red_flags.description: Two-week crescendo of exertional chest tightness (new-onset, worsening angina)
red_flags.clinical_significance: New-onset, accelerating angina is by definition unstable angina — an acute coronary syndrome.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise the escalating pattern and its emergency implication.

red_flags.description: First-degree relative with premature myocardial infarction / sudden cardiac death at a young age
red_flags.clinical_significance: Powerful pre-test-probability multiplier for coronary disease and a hallmark of Familial Hypercholesterolaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Loss of the single strongest contextual signal that this young patient is genuinely high-risk.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing serious cardiac disease because the patient is only 34 — premature CAD from FH presents exactly this way, and age must not down-rank exertional jaw-radiating chest pain.
Attributing the pain to a panic attack because the patient is visibly distressed, rather than recognising that the distress is a reaction to genuine cardiac symptoms.
Settling on a benign cause (reflux, muscle strain from lifting groceries) and stopping the work-up before excluding ACS.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Diagnosing the chronic condition instead of the acute threat
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing on confirming Familial Hypercholesterolaemia (which needs bloods/genetics and is not the emergency) instead of escalating the immediate suspected ACS.
Being reassured by near-normal patient-reported vitals or partial symptom relief — neither excludes ACS, which requires an ECG and troponin.

risk_scores.tool_name: Dutch Lipid Clinic Network (DLCN) criteria (for FH, retrospective)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Likely 'probable/definite FH' given tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus <45y, premature personal CAD and family history — pending LDL value
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a clinical diagnosis of Familial Hypercholesterolaemia driving premature CAD; confirms long-term high cardiovascular risk. Not an acute triage tool.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG for ischaemic changes (ST elevation/depression, T-wave inversion)
Auscultation of heart sounds and assessment for heart-failure signs
Palpation of Achilles tendon xanthomas and formal examination for corneal arcus and xanthelasma
Fasting lipid profile / LDL cholesterol and genetic testing to confirm FH
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise possible acute coronary syndrome and call 000 for an emergency ambulance without delay; instruct the patient to chew a single 300 mg aspirin if it is available and they are not allergic, stay completely at rest, and not walk or drive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is UNDER-action — reassuring a young, anxious patient with 'probably muscular/reflux/anxiety' instead of escalating. The FH diagnosis itself cannot and need not be confirmed remotely.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Undifferentiated acute chest pain with features suggestive of cardiac ischaemia requires urgent assessment with ECG and troponin; give aspirin 300 mg unless contraindicated and arrange emergency transfer.

Tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus before age 45, premature CAD and a family history of premature MI indicate probable Familial Hypercholesterolaemia; confirm with lipid profile and consider genetic testing and cascade screening of relatives, and treat with high-intensity statin.
